chunks.text: Where's our green scope? We don't know who. Don't worry. They'll be here. Doesn't matter. Okay. All right. Nice to see you, and thank you for coming. Now, we've got five minutes with you. You're our first submitter. You've done this before. Once. Once. And for anyone listening, everyone has read the papers, so you don't need to laboriously go through the papers. What would be helpful, especially with only five minutes, leave a little bit of time for one question also.

Okay. All right. Fantastic. I'm just trying to give you a bit of advice, but more generally to anyone submitting this morning. Thank you. Thank you. Are you ready? You're... Please proceed, John. Ah, thank you. Thanks for giving me this opportunity to speak to my submission. When I think about the increasingly punitive incarceration policies of this coalition, more people locked up, disenfranchisement of all prisoners, and now this amendment that will allow, it seems to me, longer periods of solitary confinement, I ask myself five basic questions.

Why is New Zealand's incarceration rate so much higher than Australia's or England's, double Canada's, and double Ireland's? And why is the government planning and budgeting to increase incarceration still further by more than a third, and women by two thirds?

Why is our recidivism rate so high? 60% within two years of release. Obviously, we're managing rehabilitation and reintegration very badly. Will more incarceration and allowing prisoners to be kept in solitary for 13 days make for a safer society?

I think not, or we wouldn't have to keep locking up more and more people. Common sense tells us that to keep doing the same thing over and over and expecting a different result is unsound, to put it kindly. Are the billions of dollars being spent locking up prisoners the way we do an efficient use of our tax dollars?

Again, obviously not. If it were, our incarceration and recidivism rates would be going down, and they're not. And do punitive measures such as solitary confinement and disenfranchisement help towards rehabilitation and reintegration? That one's rhetorical.

For several years, up until COVID, I was a volunteer in Rimutaka Prison. I worked with prisoners across all security levels. In the high security pods, I saw it firsthand the effect of lockdowns and short staffing problems, when prisoners might get an hour out of their cells a day, and it was not good.

At such times, they'd come to literacy lessons for an hour out of their cell, even if it was schoolwork, but in no state to learn. No one could say this was conducive to rehabilitation. In contrast, where effective things were happening, such as the few trade training courses, the difference was stark.

These students were proud of their achievements. They had hopes of a job on release. More than one said to me, "This is my last lag." New Zealand has ratified the Convention Against Torture and Other Cruel, Inhuman or Degrading Treatment or Punishment. We've designated national preventative measures, including the Office of the Ombudsman and the Human Rights Commission, to make recommendations to government on these issues, and they do.

In his report after visiting a maximum security peru unit 18 months ago, then Ombudsman Peter Boshier stated unequivocally, "I saw compelling evidence of prolonged solitary confinement in oppressive conditions of all those in custody in the peru, as well as other human rights abuses, including concerning incidents of the use of force. I consider the cumulative human impact of the conditions and treatment of the people in custody in the peru to be cruel, inhuman and degrading." The Ombudsman's own words.

And now the coalition wants to extend incarceration and solitary confinement provisions. Locking up prisoners in solitary may be an easy option for prisoner management in the short term, but many studies have shown the serious psychoses that result from it. If prolonged, these psychoses become irreversible, hard-baked into prisoners who will eventually be released into society. It's not rehabilitation, and it certainly isn't preparation for reintegration.

I'm asking the select committee to please think seriously about the effect this amendment will have, and to advise the government to withdraw it in its current form. Thank you. Okay. We're just about out of time, but we'll have one question of anyone. Right. Dr. Tracey McLennan. Thank you. I just wanted to commend the work that you've done.

Oh, thank you. It's not easy, and it's not everybody's first priority, and it makes a huge-... difference, so I want to acknowledge that. You mentioned training, and I just wanted to know, can you give me an example of the types of positive kind of- Well, the one I saw that I don't think it's happening anymore, they built a house from scratch, from the ground up.

And this was in the lower security area inside the wire. And the people working on it did Everything, from the pouring of- yeah, everything, including painting and paper hanging. And one of them that I was doing NCEA with said to me, "I was working out there the other day, and a group from the industry came in. One of them tapped me on the shoulder and said, 'I have a job for you when you come out.'"

Mm. And that was so positive. Thank you, Mr. Bonnett, for turning up and particularly in person. It's always much more compelling. Thank you very much. Cool. Right. Now, Max Marshall. He's on Zoom, Max. He's on Zoom. I thought he was going to be here in person. Oh.

Mr. Marshall, if you're there, we're ready for you. All right. We were looking forward to seeing you this morning, but obviously other plans took hold. Yeah. That's all right. Sorry about- No, that's all right. I- Hey, we've got five minutes with you, Max.

Okay. Good stuff. Good morning, everyone. Thank you for giving me the opportunity to speak. What I want to do is talk to you about the concept of dangerousness with respect to one particular subcategory inside the profile of dangerous sexual offenders, that of the minor attracted individual, whose interest has a biological dimension.

For some minor attracted individuals experienced in law, the guarantees of natural justice have not prevailed. I am interested in what shapes the concern for this group in prison. Much controversy arose that occurs around decisions for future safety of public.

And again, I want to stress the randomness of law cannot reconcile risk and capable risk. Some historical context. The gay liberation movement fight was framed around sexual consent. Prior to law reform, public toilets were used as meeting places where men had sex with other men, and among these encounters were many with young people. The police's attempt to block centered around the use of this public space other than the use intended. Interesting to note, assumptions were that the young person would not be having sex with

the older person. Rather, the police saw it only as part of the gay lifestyle career. The move to remove many public toilets came at the same time as law reform. And while it gave us the appearance of becoming a more liberal society, at the same time, the boundary between sex between an adult and a young person today has become a further intense boundary, one which must not be crossed. And while we agree with the sentiment born this way should mean precisely that, the profile of a minor attracted person is too hard for many.

Even more difficult is the idea of a young person having agency in these situations. The therapy for those incarcerated follows two forms. The first one attempts to get the person to see their sexuality as erroneous. The second attempts to conform his or her sexuality to appear more normal to society.

I argue that this is bad therapy. Both stigmatize rather than provide help. What has not been explored is how the experience of the inmate as an individual critiques and challenges assumptions about what the content of what normal involves.

This insistence that of just deserves sentencing is part of an extended process, which begins when the police and prosecution decide on how to process suspects. The application of the term dangerous for this profile is unfair, if not prejudicial. Where punishments may appear to statutes of law, many key decisions are not visible. The challenge presented by this group pose no easy answers, but failure is guaranteed for the process of developing solutions if we are automatically going to view this group as dangerous.

But this is what shapes many lawmakers and society at large. All right. Hey, thank you, Max. Very nice to see you again, and thank you again for your contribution. All right? Right. We are moving on to the New Zealand Law Society, which are via Zoom. Hey, good morning, Kerry. How are you going?

Are you on your own? You got Matt with you? Oh, here you are. Cool. Right then, we've got 10 minutes with you. Good morning. Thank you, sir. Yes, so, I'm Matt McKillop from the Human Rights and Privacy Committee of the Law Society, and with Mr. Cook from the Criminal Law Committee.

Thank you for the opportunity to speak to our submission in this matter. I'll start by saying that there's a lot of good in this bill, and I don't want to-- just because we're going to be focusing on a big issue, I don't want to skip that. We support the bill's intention to establish a clear statutory basis for the safe, humane, and lawful management of this small group of very high-risk prisoners.

We do, however, have serious concerns as to some aspects of the proposals , principally That if adopted, the amendments would sanction treatment that falls below international human rights norms. I won't be repeating our written submission, but will simply start by drawing the committee's attention to those main human rights issues that we would suggest require change. And I'll then hand over to Mr. Cook, who'll speak about some other proposed drafting improvements, which will focus on just one or two of the most essential ones in this oral submission.

So, to start with those human rights concerns, the Law Society supports the creation of this statutory process for designated management prisoners. The present lack of any statutory process for identifying people in this group has been a subject of criticism by the Chief Ombudsman, the Chief Inspector of Corrections.

However, as our submission outlines, the provision in the bill for minimum meaningful human contact has some problems. This, I'm talking about the rule that would be set out in new Section 69A, and that's opposed by the Law Society in its present form.

The reasons for that are as follows. At international law, the concepts of solitary confinement, prolonged solitary confinement, and meaningful human contact emerged from the Mandela Rules, as they're called for short. Those rules are an instrument adopted by the UN General Assembly as a group of universal norms relating to how prisons ought to be run.
But it's not just a UN

aspiration, it's also expressly integrated into our act in Section Five of the Principal Act as one of the purposes of our regime. The bill, as drafted, would not be achieving Mandela rules consistency, and that's really the point that we make at paragraph 4.17 of our submission, that the 10-hour meaningful human contact in any 14-day period would be less than half of what would be expected in order to meet those

standards. And that base level in the Mandela Rules is around avoiding harm to detainees. We've also noted that we think Cabinet initially made a call that it should be the Mandela Rules level, and that seems to have changed without too much reconsideration. And I just wanted to note that while Peru Unit was created to house a particular serious offender, he's not representative of these prisoners as a

whole. These are often serious offenders who have had very traumatic early lives, involving their own physical or sexual abuse, gang upbringings, often also have mental health or intellectual disability or other sorts of issues that contribute to their risk. And socializing these guys at a level that falls below the minimum level in the Mandela Rules risks further harm to these prisoners.

So, I'm rushing through here because of the time. I'll hand over to Mr. Cook now to pick that up. Morning. The Law Society believes that our submissions have some essential matters that really could sharpen the drafting of the designation test and strengthen other aspects of the proposed new regime, and these include the addition of "imminent" to the proposed test of extreme risk, i.e., an imminent extreme risk would be preferable. Our submission discusses this at 3.8 and 3.11.

The Law Society further suggests that the designation test could require the chief executive to determine that the imminent extreme risk to prison or public safety cannot be mitigated by ordinary management measures reasonably available to corrections, and can only be properly addressed by designation and restrictive management.

Restrictive management, with all that it entails, should only be considered if the risks of that particular prisoner cannot be sufficiently mitigated by less intrusive and rights infringing conduct. We also recommend that the test for designation in sections 52 [C] and 52 is too broad, should be revised to solely respond to extreme risk that is present whilst a person is detained in the prison. There will be prisoners who pose an extreme risk of serious offending if released from prison, but who do not pose any risk when managed in custody subject to the usual regime applying in that prison

unit. And that group is not the bill's concern and do not warrant the restrictions or resources. Also, the new section 52K sub one in clause 12 of the bill creates a duty for the department to inform the chief executive if the department becomes aware of information sufficient to justifying a review of the designation.

Whilst it's relatively common to define the functions of public service agencies in statute, it seems unusual to confer a duty on a department rather than a natural or legal person. Reformulating the duty as one held by the chief executive would be preferable, and we've also raised the question of designation of persons under 25. If provision to do so is retained, should it be a different test? The committee may wish to consider this aspect.

And our submission raises other points relating to segregation, prisoners' trust accounts, and legal representation. But Mr. McKillop or myself are happy to answer any questions should you have any in the very short time you have available. Good. I've got one from Mr. Dan Rosewarne. Thanks, Chair. I just want to turn to your comment around roles of the panel and the chief executive.

In paragraph 3.19 of your submission, you raise a situation where the chief executive departs from the panel's recommendation. I just want to get a sense of-... how real that litigation risk is. And can you think of a scenario where the CE would not follow the panel's recommendation? I'm happy to answer this.

So the reason that we have included that is because it does put the independence of the decision-maker as the CE, he is independent, and there may be situations in which he takes a different view, listening to his staff rather than the panel. That is problematic because it does create a litigation risk, and given that this is in a situation where the rights are going to be zealously guarded by individuals that have very little rights, we think that litigation risk is real.

Why did you suggest putting the word imminent? If someone's an extreme risk and clearly must have done something to be deemed to be an extreme risk, why do you have to put a present tense of imminent in front of it? I cannot understand why you're recommending that.

So a risk that's distant or speculative may never materialize or may be overtaken by intervening events. The closer a risk is to occurring, the more reliably you can assess whether it will actually happen. The more imminent a risk, the less time there is to monitor, reassess, or apply lesser interventions. Imminence therefore justifies acting now rather than waiting for more information. There's also proportionality issues.

A remote risk may not warrant intervention. The cost or burden of the response would outweigh the benefit. Imminence tests the balance- But if a prisoner has already exhibited actions that are reprehensible, and I'm not just talking about a certain individual, but have done that, and they've clearly been classified because of what they've done.

Why would you then say they have to be imminent? Because if a person has got to that stage, they've been deemed, because there's very few people deemed that way. Well, imminence also requires discipline in terms of the analysis. It prevents a remote or theoretical risk from being used to justify disproportionate action today, and that's particularly important in human rights law. Ultimately, if that person poses a risk, then the imminence of that risk is going to be an important factor in determining whether or not you want to

impose these very- Can I rephrase the question?... significant restrictions. Mr. Cook, I wonder if I can ask that a different way, because imminence in its kind of natural language makes it sound like they're on the cusp of violence at every moment.

When you say imminent, do you simply mean that the risk of serious violence or an incident is real and immediate and ongoing rather than historic? Yes. So they're not being judged merely upon maybe the actions that resulted in the imprisonment, but it's an ongoing risk that has some immediacy about it.

Okay. All right, cool. Hey, thank you to both of you for joining us this morning. Thank you for the invitation. Very helpful. Yeah. All right, cool. Thank you. All right, thank you. Thanks very much. See you both. All right. I think we've got Amanda Hill. Right. I saw you come in, so please come over here. We've got 10 minutes with you.

Kia ora koutou. My name is Amanda Hill. With me is Farroz Broushman- Farroz... who's a lawyer in my firm. Thank you for your time today. My practice focuses on public law cases, which often involve people who are detained. And I do that work because I have an underlying belief that prisons can be lawfully managed in a way that promotes rehabilitation and also meets our desire for punishment, which is a really natural human sentiment. And those two things don't have to be mutually exclusive. So that's how I wanted to start out.

There are two buckets, rehabilitation and lawful treatment, and punishment, and they don't take away from each other. I was here to listen to the Law Society submissions, and I'd like to endorse the things I heard there. And I'm not going to repeat those, as Mr.

McKillop stole my point about Section 5 of the Corrections Act, bringing the Mandela Rules into our legislation, and I'm not going to rehash that ground, and I'm not going to go over our written submissions. I just think it is important that when we draft legislation, it is in accordance with the things that we've signed up to at United Nations level, and that goes to our baseline reputation as an international actor and someone who sticks with what we sign up for, in short.

My starting point is that this bill shouldn't progress. That it seeks to implement what is already existing in the PRU, which is found to be unlawful, rather than try to elevate the PRU to something that meets our legal obligations. However, I'm also a realist. I recognize that this bill may pass if it goes through the whole process within this parliamentary session. And reflecting on that, we'll also try and suggest some amendments to the bill that might try and improve it.

One of the key things, and I think the Law Society dealt with this, is the issue of meaningful human contact. How much should a person have, and what is the quality of that? The bill currently provides for around a third of the international obligation of two hours of meaningful human contact a day.

So it's a third of what we've signed up to at the UN. It also decreases the quality of that contact. Best practice and expert opinion is that meaningful human contact is in person like we are here now, not with barriers, not over the phone, that we become better people when we're in a room with each other.

My clients are in the PRU. I have eight clients who are either in the PRU or have been in the PRU. They forget how to talk. They forget how to converse. They forget how to read body language. And when you are talking about risk of violence, an inability to read body language, an inability to talk, those go to creating risk.

So I'd like to just remind you of what the importance of being in a room. And so we say that if you are going to have this type of unit, then the legislation must match the two hours requirement in the Mandela Rules. Anything less sets a floor that is less than our obligations.

And it has to ensure that there is good quality, meaningful contact. And a lot of the other provisions might limit that. So, cell adjacent yards remove meaningful human contact. So some of those provisions put a dent in the ways in which people can be together. And I think that it's important to make sure that the legislation reflects those things.

We want to talk a bit about what we call the entry and exit to the PRU, because I think there is some amendments that would be really helpful there to ensure that there are robust processes. But one thing I want to touch on before I hand over to Mr. Brailsford, because I want to leave some time for questions. I'm one of the few people that have been in the PRU, and I can talk a lot about it.

I think I'm able to answer some questions that you might have. But one of my baseline requirements, and one of the things I think is really important, that this bill should provide that any person who has a diagnosed mental illness or an intellectual disability should not be put in the prisons of extreme risk unit. It is very clear, and our own Supreme Court has found that if you place someone with known mental health vulnerabilities or intellectual disabilities in the PRU, that meets the definition of torture when they are under prolonged solitary

confinement. There's no leeway there. If you take someone with those disabilities, you are immediately in breach. So I just want to be really clear, and that goes to what assessments and what evidence we have or what the decision maker has before them when they are looking at should someone go into this regime.

I will pause there and have Farros talk about the changes we'd suggest to entry and exit to the PRU. All right, cool. I've got a couple of questions. Certainly. Yeah. So the first thing is shifting it from the panel and the chief executive to the High Court.

Adopting the approach that we have around public protection orders and ESOs, those are the types of mechanisms we're using to manage extreme or ongoing imminent risk. And it's appropriate for those decisions to be made in a really independent way, and the High Court offers that and offers some rigor to that decision making.

Again, I won't take you to those provisions, but I think that's to be strongly preferred over, as the Law Society recognized, a panel advising a statutory officer, and it just starts to get murky in terms of where the clarity and submissions from the person subject to those orders might go. The next is around notifications, and I talk about this in one particular place in our written submissions, but it also applies in relation to the destruction of property.

I think the provisions could just be strengthened to make clear that the reasons given to a prisoner enable them to challenge or fully understand the decision being made or the potential decision to be made. And that's based on some of the other notification requirements that we see in the Corrections Act, where there are reasons given, but really only in summary and not sufficient for someone to fully understand how that decision's been reached.

And given the significance of these types of orders, it's important that the person subject to them understands them fully. Okay, cool. Right, I'm going to go to Mr- I'm happy to leave. Do you want to do it? Tracy McLellan. Dr. Tracy McLellan. Thank you. I just wanted a little bit more information about that judicial decision making. So is that comparable to other types of decisions that would always be made in a judicial way as opposed to a panel?

Can you give me an example just to help me orient that? So my understanding is that with public protection orders, which is the Essentially detention of someone that served their sentence in a prison or prison-like setting, those would always be made by the High Court. In terms of an ESO, an extended supervision order, which is extending parole-like conditions for someone that presents a risk post-release, but not the same level of risk as necessitating, I guess, continued detention under a PPO.

That is made either by the district or High Court, depending on the nature of the offense for which they were convicted. So just for clarity, can you think of any other kind of decision that would be made in this proposed way that's of kind of an equivalent importance? Do we make decisions via the CE and, for something that you would see as having the same kind of litigation risk or the same importance?

Decisions around placement in prisons are often considered to be the purview of the chief executive, but they're often delegated. Mm-hmm. And so senior advisors or regional managers, or other people make those decisions, and currently, decisions about the parole are made by the commissioner. So there's no sunlight on that.

So there are... Sorry. I would add, I think it's really general placement in terms of the unit or prison. Those, I don't think are really analogous to placement in the parole, because if you're shifting someone from a low security unit to a high security unit because of conduct, that is really quite operational. You're not significantly changing the regime to which they're subject in the way that you are when you're subjecting them to a directed restricted management.

All right. Hey, thank you very much for coming this morning and presenting. I really appreciate that. Could I have 30 seconds to just one more suggested amendment. In your segregation orders, there's a proposal for a five-day effectively slip rule. I think that should be removed. Segregation orders should be read strictly. And, the five-day rule, I would suggest, would be an amendment that would be appropriate to take that part out.

All right. Thank you. Thank you very much. Great. Right. People Against Prisons. I think Ruth is on the line, is that right? Good morning. How are you? I'm well, thanks. Morana, can you hear me all right? We can, and we've got you for 10 minutes. All right, great.

Tena koutou katoa. I'm Ruth, and I'm submitting on behalf of People Against Prisons Aotearoa. We are a prison abolitionist organization working for a safer, fairer Aotearoa. Thank you for having me. This is my second time submitting to select committee on behalf of PAPA and my first time submitting to the Justice Committee.

Today, I'm going to highlight a few parts of our full submission, the inadequate standard for meaningful human contact, and the introduction of a very broad definition for segregating prisoners. I missed the Law Society submission but have picked up from the following submitters that the Mandela Rules have already been named and discussed as something that submitters want to see for our country.

And I will touch on those, but not in great depth because it sounds like those have been covered. We do note that some parts of the bill, and especially its overall stated intent, is to reduce harm to designated management prisoners. However, the designations that are proposed really lack transparency. And most of the bill reinforces a prison system which gives too much discretionary power to corrections staff without independent oversight.

So those are the things that we're concerned about and call on the committee to reject. Hmm. We also do understand that some of the people being kept in solitary confinement or as designated management prisoners are not particularly sympathetic or, as I think the previous submitter said, have behaviors that do require management and a different level of approach, of operational approach.

However, we don't believe this justifies maltreatment and torture. Those tools are not rehabilitative, and they don't contribute to the wellbeing of the people who are kept in prisons. So to focus on our two specific places from our written submission, the minimum meaningful human contact in the bill is absolutely inadequate.

It must be strengthened to 14 hours per week in line with the international standards set by the Mandela rules. These global standards are adopted by the United Nations, and we call on the committee to uphold the same standard here in this country. As we all know, solitary confinement increases the risk of suicide and self-harm, and it is classified as torture internationally. The bill requires that prisoners receive only 10 hours of meaningful contact in every 14-day period, and this is 30% less than the global standard.

And while the bill names the global standard as desirable, it doesn't propose it as a legal requirement. This is inadequate. A non-binding aspiration does not provide meaningful protection for prisoners against the harms of solitary confinement. It also decreases their chance of rehabilitation back into society. We also have the same concerns as other submitters have talked about with the bill's definition of meaningful human contact, which includes telephone call.

The kind of contact that really protects a person from psychological harms of isolation requires that in-person face-to-face interaction. The comments made by the previous submitter around critical exposure to other humans in order to retain the ability to read body language and emotional expressions is exactly what we're talking about. And furthermore, the bill itself acknowledges that phone calls are inadequate by noting that it is desirable for a prisoner to have contact not only by telephone call.

So again, this is another place where we call on the committee to require this as a legal requirement rather than a non-binding aspiration. So we call on the committee to amend Section 69 of the proposed bill to require 14 hours of meaningful face-to-face contact which cannot be met by telephone. The second area I wanted to draw attention to from our written proposal are the grounds for segregation. These new grounds are dangerously broad, and they can easily be used to punish prisoners.

So the three new grounds that the bill proposes to introduce do not require that any offending or harm has actually occurred. They require only that a prison manager forms the opinion that harm might occur. This is extremely discretionary and very dangerous in nature without the transparent oversight and the checks and balances. It allows subjective opinions of an individual to influence the welfare of a prisoner.

We're strongly opposed to these provisions. The existing segregation grounds already give corrections staff significant discretion. These new additions extend that discretion into territory that is inherently subjective. And we're also aware, through our support networks of those who are incarcerated, that prisoners who do use the proper channels to formally seek their rights be met are often segregated and/or transferred between prisons as a form of punishment. So since we know this practice is already happening, we

see the new definitions of segregation as another step in this direction, allowing the discretion of corrections staff to determine who is segregated when. The previous submitter discussed the importance of a prisoner knowing the basis on which they were segregated into DMP status. But based on what we already know is happening, increasing this definition will only worsen the situation of providing the insufficient information for the people who actually are incarcerated on what decisions have been made around them.

So segregation is already happening in our prisons. It does not need to be expanded. We call on the committee to reject the new segregation grounds in the Section 58 (1) (c) and in their entirety. In closing, the bill does not match its stated intent. It reduces rather than increases protection for designated management prisoners. It puts them at greater risk of psychological harm, and it does not put our country into the global standard for treatment of prisoners.

Thank you for your time, and I'm happy to try and answer any questions that I'm able to. Okay. All right. Thanks, Ruth. We've got a question from Rima Nagli. Kia ora, Ruth. You began your submission by saying that your organization is a prison abolitionist organization. Can you please explain what that is in plain English?

Yeah. So a prison abolitionist organization, we believe that the treatment of prisoners and wrongdoing in society is not made better by the way that our prisons are run and the treatment of individuals in the prisons. While we do support the public safety, we do not see the current prison system as contributing to that long term because of the cyclical nature of how people receive more damage on the inside than they do.

So thank you, Ruth. So just a quick supplementary. So the abolitionist word, do you propose that prisons be abolished? We do not propose that prisons be abolished overnight, tomorrow. We simply- But at some stage... we simply see them as an institution that causes more harm than it does good at a long term basis.

More harm for whom? All right. Okay. All right. Hey, very good. Thank you for joining us for your second submission. You're becoming a seasoned professional. Abolitionist Thanks, Ruth. Enjoyed your- Thank you... presentation this morning. Right. Now- With the bill made... Te Hunga Rua, we've got... Are they on soon?
Sorry.

Good morning. How are you, Alex? Morning. Oh, good. Are you on your own this morning? Yes, I am. Yeah, cool. Right. We've got 10 minutes with you. Thank you. To say at the outset, Te Hunga Rua Maori o Aotearoa recognizes the stated intent of the bill but submits that it requires amendment before it could be considered to be passed in law. If I can focus primarily on the

recommendations that we've put in our submission. Much like some of the other submitters, we submit it needs to provide clearer statutory thresholds, and evidential requirements in order to consider someone for a unit of this type that's been spoken about in the various material for this bill, and the restrictive management that it then applies to prisoners.

One of the particular parts around that is the minimum requirements of meaningful human contact, and as the previous submitter spoke about, at the very least aligning that to the international standard, and to ensure that this doesn't result in material isolation.

In our submission, it should be made clear that this approach is a matter of last resort. Whilst we accept that there are some prisoners that will require more intensive management, this shouldn't be an option that's taken lightly in our submission because of the potential for harmful impact that it has on rehabilitation and reintegration into society.

There is, in our submission, an absence of, or a lack of, Tikanga-informed rehabilitation processes where much of Tikanga relies on social interaction.
